Member-only story
Anthropic Launches
Claude Managed
Agents (That Make
Agentic AI Workflows
Real)
Joe Njenga Following 7 min read · Just now
Open in app
Search Write
Claude Managed Agents stops you from wasting
time trying to string scripts together to make your
agents work.
I just tested the new Claude Managed Agents and
discovered the missing link in AI agent workflows.
First, if you have been working with AI agents or
building them, you know the common problem.
You spend more time working on the logic than on
the product.
The agent loop, tool execution, context management,
sandboxing, and session handling; all of it falls on
you to wire up from scratch before a single line of
real logic gets written.
When something breaks mid-task, you are back to
debugging infrastructure instead of shipping.
That is the problem Claude Managed Agents
promises to solve. Instead of giving us another API to
wrap your loop around, Anthropic is now handing us
the runtime.
This is a fully managed cloud environment where
Claude reads files, runs commands, browses the
web, and executes code on its own.
Session continuity and context management are
already handled. I took my time to fully review and
see if it lives up to the promise.
Claude Managed Agents
Anthropic shipped two things :
First is Claude Managed Agents — a fully hosted
agent harness running on Anthropic’s
infrastructure. You define the agent, fire off a task,
and Anthropic’s cloud handles everything else: the
container, the tools, the execution, the session.
Second is the Claude Agent SDK (formerly the
Claude Code SDK) — a Python and TypeScript
library that embeds the same agent loop powering
Claude Code into your own application. You host it
yourself, but all the hard parts come pre-built.
Both solve the same problem, but the difference is
where the agent runs.
SDK — agent runs inside your own infrastructure
Managed Agents — agent runs on Anthropic’s
cloud, fully managed
For most developers reading this, Managed Agents is
the more significant release, and for this article, we
will focus on Clause Managed Agents.
Four Building Blocks
Managed Agents is built around four concepts.
Once you understand these, the whole system
clicks:
Your application sends events to a session running inside Anthropic’s
cloud, where the agent and environment are already configured
Agent — your configuration: model, system prompt,
tools, MCP servers, and skills. Define it once,
reference it by ID across every session.
Environment — a cloud container with pre-installed
packages (Python, Node.js, Go, and more), network
access rules, and mounted files. This is the
workspace your agent operates in.
Session — a running instance of your agent inside
an environment, executing a specific task. Files,
conversation history, and context all persist across
its lifetime.
Events — the messages flowing between your
application and the agent: user turns, tool results,
status updates, and streamed responses.
Here’s how it looks in code. You create an agent once:
import anthropic
client = anthropic.Anthropic()
# Create the agent — do this once, reuse by ID
agent = client.beta.agents.create(
model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
system="You are a software engineer. Fix bugs, ru
tools=[{"type": "bash"}, {"type": "file_operation
name="bug-fixer"
print(agent.id)  # Save this — you'll reference it ac
Then start a session and send a task:
# Create an environment with the packages your agent
environment = client.beta.environments.create(
packages=["pytest", "requests"]
# Start a session referencing your agent and environm
session = client.beta.sessions.create(
agent_id=agent.id,
environment_id=environment.id
# Send your task as an event
response = client.beta.sessions.events.create(
session_id=session.id,
content="Find and fix the failing tests in auth.p
That’s the complete setup, which includes one agent
definition, the environment, and the session; then
Claude takes it from there.
How the Agent Loop Works
When you start a session, Claude doesn’t respond
once and wait.
It runs a continuous loop that goes like this: receive
prompt, evaluate the task, call tools, process results,
repeat; until the job is done. Each full round trip is
one turn.
Claude evaluates, calls tools, processes results, and repeats each turn
autonomously until the task is complete or a limit is reached
Here’s what that looks like for a real task: “find and
fix the failing tests in auth.py.”
Turn 1 — Claude runs the test suite via Bash. Three
failures come back.
Turn 2 — Claude reads auth.py the test file to
understand the problem.
Turn 3 — Claude edits the file and re-runs the tests.
All three pass.
Final turn — Claude returns a text response with no
tool calls. Task complete.
Here’s how that session looks in code:
import anthropic
client = anthropic.Anthropic()
async for event in client.beta.sessions.stream(
session_id=session.id,
event={
"type": "user",
"content": "Find and fix the failing tests in
# See what Claude is doing each turn
if event.type == "assistant_message":
print(event.content)
# Capture the final result
if event.type == "result":
print(f"Done: {event.result}")
print(f"Turns used: {event.num_turns}")
print(f"Cost: ${event.total_cost_usd:.4f}")
session_id = event.session_id  # save to resu
This stream gives you full visibility: every tool call,
result, and turn as it happens in real time.
Context Management
One thing that breaks most DIY agent setups is
context.
Every turn adds to the context window: the prompt,
tool definitions, file reads, command outputs, and
conversation history. Once you hit the limit, the
session dies.
Managed Agents handles this automatically.
When the context window approaches its limit, the
system compacts the conversation by summarizing
older history to free up space while keeping recent
exchanges and key decisions intact.
This approach allows the session to continue
without interruption.
Three Controls
Effort level — controls how deeply Claude reasons
on each turn.
Set it deliberately since it affects token usage and
cost.
Max turns and budget — cap the loop before it
runs away.
session = client.beta.sessions.create(
agent_id=agent.id,
environment_id=environment.id,
max_turns=20,           # stop after 20 tool-use
max_budget_usd=0.50     # or when spend hits $0.5
When either limit is hit, the session returns a clear
result subtype so you know why it stopped, and
you can resume with the session ID if needed.
Permission mode — controls what Claude can do
without asking.
default — gates anything not pre-approved through
your callback
acceptEdits — auto-approves file edits, still gates
shell commands
bypassPermissions — runs everything without
prompting, for isolated containers and CI only
You Can Steer It Mid-Task
While a session is running, you can send
additional events to redirect it, add context, or stop
it.
# Send a mid-session correction
client.beta.sessions.events.create(
session_id=session.id,
content="Actually, only fix the auth_token tests
If Claude is heading the wrong direction, you don’t
wait for it to finish.
Sessions are also persistent; you can capture the
session ID, and you can resume where you left off.
# Resume a previous session
async for event in client.beta.sessions.stream(
session_id="sess_abc123",   # from a previous run
event={"type": "user", "content": "Continue — now
if event.type == "result":
print(event.result)
Claude Managed Agent Tools
One of the biggest time-wasters when building
agents manually is tool execution.
Writing the wrappers, handling errors, and
managing results back into context. With Managed
Agents, that work is already done.
Here’s the full toolset:
All built-in tools available in Claude Managed Agents, grouped by
category
File operations
Read — Read any file in the container
Write — create new files
Edit — modify existing files
Search
Glob — find files by pattern
Grep — search content with regex
Execution
Bash — run shell commands, scripts, git operations,
install packages, run tests
Web
WebSearch — search the internet
WebFetch — fetch and parse full page content from
any URL
Orchestration
Task — spawn subagents for isolated work
Skill — invoke reusable workflows
AskUserQuestion — pause and request human input
TodoWrite — track multi-step work across a session
Beyond the built-ins, you connect external services
via MCP servers and define custom tools with your
own handlers.
Getting Started
Claude Managed Agents is currently in beta.
Access is enabled by default for all API accounts; if
you already have a Claude API key, you can start.
Here’s what you need:
A Claude API key from platform.claude.com
The managed-agents-2026-04-01 beta header on all
requests
Python or TypeScript SDK — the SDK sets the header
automatically
Install the SDK:
pip install anthropic
Set your API key:
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=your-api-key
Your first working agent in under 20 lines:
import anthropic
import asyncio
client = anthropic.Anthropic()
async def main():
agent = client.beta.agents.create(
model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
system="You are a helpful engineering assista
tools=[{"type": "bash"}, {"type": "file_opera
name="my-first-agent"
environment = client.beta.environments.create(
packages=["pytest"]
session = client.beta.sessions.create(
agent_id=agent.id,
environment_id=environment.id,
max_turns=10,
max_budget_usd=0.25
async for event in client.beta.sessions.stream(
session_id=session.id,
event={"type": "user", "content": "List all P
if event.type == "result":
print(event.result)
print(f"Cost: ${event.total_cost_usd:.4f}
asyncio.run(main())
Three features
Outcomes
Multi-agent coordination,
Persistent memory
All are in research preview and require a separate
access request at claude.com/form/claude-managed-
agents.
Final Thoughts
Claude Managed Agents is a brilliant idea; the
agent loop, context management, tool execution,
and session continuity.
What used to be weeks of infrastructure work is now
a configuration file and an API call. That’s a great
step in building production agents fast.
The trade-off is that the quality of your output still
depends on the quality of your input.
Have you tried Claude Managed Agents, and what’s
your experience? Let me know your thoughts in the
comments below.

OpenAI harness engineering: AI agents generating pull requests with
zero human-written code, progressive disclosure architecture
Member-only story
OpenAI’s Harness
Engineering
Experiment: Zero
Manually-Written Code
Open in app
Search Write
Based on OpenAI’s “Harness engineering:
leveraging Codex in an agent-first world” and
Rohit’s harness framework
Rick Hightower Following 22 min read · 4 days ago
Part 5 of the Harness Engineering Series
What if you could ship over a million lines of code
without writing a single line yourself? Discover how
OpenAI’s groundbreaking “zero manual code”
experiment transformed engineers into harness
architects, revolutionizing the approach to software
development! 🚀  #HarnessEngineering
#AIEngineering
Summary: OpenAI conducted an experiment
where over one million lines of code were
generated entirely by AI agents without any
human-written code. This was achieved through
harness engineering, which involved creating a
structured repository, ensuring application
legibility, and implementing mechanical
architecture enforcement. The engineers shifted
their role from coding to designing the
environment that allows agents to produce code,
resulting in a significant increase in productivity
and quality of output.
Note on sources: This article is my synthesis of
OpenAI’s internal Codex experiment and Rohit’s
harness engineering framework. The patterns
below are extrapolated from Ryan Lopopolo’s
public writeup, not official OpenAI terminology.
OpenAI harness engineering: AI agents generating
pull requests with zero human-written code,
progressive disclosure architecture
What happens when you tell a team of engineers to
stop writing code?
Not “write less code.” Not “let agents handle the
boilerplate.” Stop writing code. Completely. Every
single line comes from an AI agent, or it does not
ship.
OpenAI ran exactly that experiment in agentic
development. Five months later, the repository
contained “on the order of a million lines of code” -
across app logic, infra, docs, and tooling - all
written by Codex agents, with OpenAI reporting 0
lines of manually-written code. The engineers
never stopped working. They worked harder than
ever. But the nature of the work changed
completely.
They stopped being programmers. They became
harness engineers.
OpenAI harness engineering
If you have been following Rohit’s harness
engineering framework guide, this is the most
extreme validation of the thesis you will find
anywhere. The model did not change during the
experiment. What changed was everything around
it. And that everything is what made a million lines
of agent-written code actually work.
This is the story of how they did it. More
importantly, it is a playbook for the five specific
harness engineering patterns that made it
possible.
The Experiment: What “Zero Manual
Code” Actually Means in Numbers
Let us be precise about what OpenAI actually did,
because the headline invites misunderstanding.
OpenAI harness engineering: Evolution of an Engineer
The scope of the claim: Over five months, a small
OpenAI team built an internal beta product used
daily inside OpenAI, with roughly a million lines
of code; all generated by Codex agents, with
OpenAI reporting 0 lines of manually-written
code for the app and its supporting systems.
Humans never directly contributed code; they
steered through prompts, repository structure,
and feedback loops.
What that rate implies: OpenAI estimates this
approach was about 10x faster than writing the
code by hand, roughly one-tenth the time of
manual development, not an incremental
improvement, but a categorical change in output.
What the engineers were doing instead: Designing
the harness. Maintaining it. Fixing it when it
broke. The harness did not build itself. It required
continuous investment.
The team did not eliminate human involvement.
They eliminated human authorship of code.
Engineers still reviewed pull requests. They still
designed systems. They still debugged failures.
They still made architectural decisions every
single day.
What they stopped doing was typing code into
editors.
Every function, every class, every configuration
file, every test was generated by AI agents. The
humans specified what to build, how to structure
it, and where it should live. The agents wrote it.
OpenAI harness engineering: Engineering roles
Look at that table carefully. Six of seven activities
still involve engineers. The one activity that
shifted, writing code, was historically where
engineers spent 60 to 70 percent of their time. That
time got reinvested into design, review, and
harness engineering.
What each role covers (and how it changes in
“Zero Manual Code”)
System design
Before (Engineers): Engineers define the core
system shape: major components, data flows,
reliability goals, and how the system meets
product requirements.
After (Engineers): Still engineer-led, but with
extra emphasis on designing for agent
execution: clear module boundaries, explicit
interfaces, and “agent-friendly” specs that can
be mechanically verified.
Architecture decisions
Before (Engineers): Engineers decide patterns and
constraints: service boundaries, dependency
direction, persistence choices, performance
budgets, and security posture.
After (Engineers): Still engineer-led, but more
decisions are encoded as enforceable rules: repo
documentation, lint rules, contract tests, and
scaffolding that prevents agents from drifting.
Writing code
Before (Engineers): Engineers implement features
and fixes directly: creating files, editing
functions, wiring dependencies, and making
incremental changes.
After (Agents): Agents generate implementation
code from specs and repo context. Engineers
focus on prompts, task decomposition, harness
updates, and ensuring the repo contains the
“source of truth” the agents need.
Code review
Before (Engineers): Engineers review logic, style,
correctness, and maintainability, and then
request changes.
After (Engineers): Engineers still review, but
review shifts toward verifying intent, spotting
systemic failure modes, and improving the
harness. Many “style and convention” concerns
are pushed into automated checks and
remediation instructions.
Debugging failures
Before (Engineers): Engineers reproduce issues,
inspect logs and traces, isolate root causes, and
patch code.
After (Engineers + Agents): Engineers and agents
collaborate. Agents run tests, read stack traces,
and iterate on fixes. Engineers handle
ambiguous failures, design-level issues, and
harness gaps that cause repeated agent
mistakes.
Writing tests
Before (Engineers): Engineers author unit,
integration, and end-to-end tests, decide what to
test, and maintain suites over time.
After (Agents): Agents generate and update tests,
especially for well-specified behavior and
regression coverage. Engineers set the testing
strategy, coverage thresholds, and contract
boundaries that tests must enforce.
Refactoring
Before (Engineers): Engineers restructure code for
readability, performance, and maintainability,
including migrations and large-scale changes.
After (Agents): Agents perform much of the
mechanical refactor work under constraints.
Engineers define the target architecture, write
migration plans, ensure safety rails exist (tests,
linters, CI), and review for correctness and risk.
The engineers did not become less valuable. They
became differently valuable. The harness is what
made the difference.
Why One Million Lines of Agent-Written
Code Is Meaningful
Before we get into the patterns, it is worth sitting
with what that number actually represents.
On the order of a million lines of code in an
internal beta product used daily inside OpenAI
that is not blog post demos. It is not toy tooling. It
is not auto-generated boilerplate that inflates line
counts.
For context: the Linux kernel contains roughly 30
million lines of code accumulated over three
decades of contributions from thousands of
developers. A mature enterprise backend might
have 500,000 to 2 million lines across its services. A
production mobile application runs 100,000 to
One million lines, generated in five months, by a
relatively small team that wrote zero of those lines
themselves, represents a production system at real
scale. It means:
Agents handled genuine complexity, not toy
tasks
The code passed human review (because
humans still reviewed PRs)
The system actually ran and shipped, not just
compiled
Quality was maintained at a level acceptable for
production use
The claim is not “agents can produce output
quickly.” The claim is “agents can produce
reviewed, production-quality output at about 10x
the rate of writing code by hand, according to
OpenAI’s estimate - if you build the harness
correctly.”
That is what the five harness engineering patterns
enable.
OpenAI harness engineering: Production Outputs
Harness Engineering Pattern 1 (from
OpenAI’s experiment): Repository as Sole
Source of Truth
Here is the first principle that makes zero-manual-
code possible, and it is brutally simple.
If it is not in the repository, the agent does not
know it exists.
As Ryan Lopopolo wrote in OpenAI’s “Harness
engineering” post: “From the agent’s point of view,
anything it can’t access in-repo effectively doesn’t
exist, so OpenAI pushed more and more context
into the repository over time.”
This sounds obvious. It is not. Think about how
much knowledge lives outside your codebase right
now. Architecture decisions in Confluence pages.
API contracts in Notion docs. Deployment
procedures in Slack threads. Onboarding
knowledge in someone’s head.
An agent cannot read your Slack. An agent cannot
attend your standup. An agent cannot absorb the
tribal knowledge that your senior engineer carries
around. The only reality an agent experiences is
the repository it operates in.
OpenAI made this explicit and absolute. Every
piece of information an agent could possibly need
to write correct code had to exist inside the
repository. If a convention was not documented in
the repo, the agent would violate it. If an API
contract was not specified in the repo, the agent
would guess wrong. If an architectural boundary
was not encoded in the repo, the agent would cross
it.
This is not a documentation initiative. This is a
survival requirement.
Why This Harness Pattern Matters at
Scale
Consider the failure mode without this pattern. At
200,000 lines per month, an agent that consistently
applies the wrong naming convention will apply it
thousands of times before anyone catches it. An
agent that misunderstands a module boundary will
cross it in dozens of files. The damage scales with
the throughput.
The repository-as-truth pattern converts
documentation from “nice to have” to “load-
bearing structure.” Every missing convention is a
defect multiplier. Every undocumented boundary
is a source of compounding errors.
OpenAI found, predictably, that the first two
months of the experiment required heavy
documentation investment before throughput
could scale. The harness had to be built before the
agents could run.
What Goes Into the Repo
The team categorized repository knowledge into
three tiers:
Foundational (monthly): Architecture decisions,
module boundaries, coding standards, naming
conventions
Operational (weekly): API contracts, data
schemas, deployment configs, environment
setup
Tactical (daily): Task-specific context, current
sprint goals, known issues, workarounds
Traditional teams can get away with sloppy
documentation because humans fill in the gaps
with institutional knowledge. Agent teams cannot.
Every gap in repository documentation becomes a
gap in agent capability.
If you have ever onboarded a new hire and thought
“they will just pick this up from the team,”
congratulations: you have identified exactly the
kind of knowledge that will break your agents.
The boundary between agent-visible and agent-
invisible knowledge is absolute. There is no “well,
the agent can probably figure it out.” Either the
knowledge is in the repo or it does not exist.
Source: OpenAI’s “Harness engineering: leveraging
Codex in an agent-first world” (Ryan Lopopolo) -
structured docs, AGENTS.md as table of contents,
and mechanical enforcement. Also informed by
Rohit’s harness engineering thread.
OpenAI harness engineering: The harness
Harness Engineering Pattern 2 (from
OpenAI’s experiment): Progressive
Disclosure via Structured Directories
Repository-as-truth creates a new problem. If you
dump every piece of knowledge into a flat
documentation folder, the agent drowns. Context
windows are large but not infinite. Relevance
matters.
OpenAI solved this with what Ryan’s post calls
progressive disclosure: agents start with a small,
stable entry point and are taught where to look
next, rather than being overwhelmed up front.
Mechanically, this means a structured directory
layout paired with a brief map file (AGENTS.md in
OpenAI’s case) that acts as a table of contents.
The directory structure is not arbitrary. It follows a
deliberate hierarchy that mirrors how agents
navigate codebases. Agents start broad and go
deep. The structure enables that navigation.
What Progressive Disclosure Looks Like
in Practice
Here is an example layout inspired by OpenAI’s
approach (OpenAI uses AGENTS.md as the entry
point and a docs/ directory as system of record; the
following is a generalized version):
repo/
├──  BRIEF.md                    # Map file: what thi
├──  ARCHITECTURE.md             # High-level system
├──  modules/
│    ├──  auth/
│    │    ├──  README.md           # Module-level conte
│    │    ├──  CONVENTIONS.md      # Module-specific pa
│    │    └──  tests/
│    ├──  billing/
│    │    ├──  README.md
│    │    ├──  CONVENTIONS.md
│    │    └──  tests/
│    └──  notifications/
│        ├──  README.md
│        ├──  CONVENTIONS.md
│        └──  tests/
└──  .linters/
├──  architecture_rules.py
└──  naming_conventions.py
OpenAI treats their AGENTS.md as a table of
contents, not a 1,000-page manual, and keeps most
knowledge in a structured docs/ directory as the
system of record. In this generalized example,
BRIEF.md plays the same role. It does not contain
exhaustive documentation. It contains just enough
context for an agent to understand the shape of the
codebase and know where to look for deeper
information. Think of it as a table of contents with
annotations.
OpenAI harness engineering: Repository as truth
What Makes a Good Brief Map
A good brief map tells the agent three things: what
the system does, where things live, and what the
current state of each component is.
# Project Brief
This is a billing platform with three core modules.
## Modules
- `modules/auth/` - Authentication and authorization.
- `modules/billing/` - Subscription management, invoi
- `modules/notifications/` - Email, SMS, and webhook
## Key Conventions
- All API endpoints follow REST conventions in ARCHIT
- Each module has its own CONVENTIONS.md for module-s
- Tests live alongside source code in each module's t
## Current State
- Auth module: stable, v2.1
- Billing module: active development, migrating from
- Notifications module: stable, v1.4
The agent reads this file first, understands the
landscape, then dives into the specific module it
needs to work in. It never needs to load the entire
codebase into context. It navigates progressively.
Why Progressive Disclosure Reduces Token
Waste
An agent working on a billing bug does not need to
load the auth module’s implementation into its
context window. With progressive disclosure, it
reads BRIEF.md, determines the billing module is
relevant, reads modules/billing/README.md and
CONVENTIONS.md, then reads only the specific
source files it needs.
OpenAI harness engineering - Progessive Disclosure Architeture PDA
Compare that to an agent that loads the entire
codebase hoping the relevant context will surface.
At 200,000 lines per month, context waste is not an
abstract concern. It is a throughput tax.
Progressive disclosure eliminates it.
This is not clever. This is just good information
architecture. But most teams do not do it because
humans can navigate messy repos through
familiarity. Agents cannot. Agents need the mess
cleaned up.
Source: OpenAI’s “Harness engineering” post on
AGENTS.md as table of contents and a structured
docs/ directory as system of record. The BRIEF.md
layout shown above is an example layout inspired
by OpenAI’s approach and Rohit’s framework, not
the exact structure OpenAI used.
Harness Engineering Pattern 3 (from
OpenAI’s experiment): Application
Legibility
Here is where OpenAI’s harness engineering gets
genuinely interesting.
Application legibility means that every running
instance of the application is fully inspectable,
fully observable, and fully isolated. The agent does
not just write code. It boots the application, tests it,
observes it, and diagnoses it.
OpenAI harness engineering - Application Legibility
This required three capabilities that most
development environments do not have.
Isolated Bootable Instances
OpenAI made the application bootable per git
worktree, so Codex could launch one instance per
change, each with its own logs and metrics. Every
agent session gets its own complete instance of the
application. Not a shared staging environment. Not
a mock. A real, running instance that the agent
controls entirely.
Why does isolation matter at this scale? Consider a
scenario where 10 agents work simultaneously on
10 different features. Without isolation, Agent A’s
half-complete database migration breaks Agent B’s
test run. Agent C’s configuration change causes
Agent D’s service to fail in a way that has nothing
to do with Agent D’s code. Every agent wastes
cycles debugging problems created by other
agents.
With isolated instances, each agent operates in a
clean environment. Its failures are its own. Its
successes are reproducible. The agents can run in
parallel without interference.
Browser Tools
Agents do not just compile and run tests. They
interact with the application the way a user would.
OpenAI wired the Chrome DevTools Protocol into
the agent runtime so Codex could drive the UI and
reason about DOM snapshots and screenshots -
navigating the UI, clicking buttons, filling forms,
and observing visual output.
This matters because many bugs are invisible to
unit tests. A function that returns correct data but
renders it in the wrong place will pass every
integration test but fail every user interaction.
Agents with browser tools catch these failures
before merge, not after deployment.
The practical implication: browser tool access
transformed agents from “code writers that hope
the code works” to “code writers that verify the
code works from the user’s perspective.”
Full Observability Stack
OpenAI set up a local observability stack per
worktree, with LogQL/PromQL access for logs and
metrics. Every agent session included structured
logging, metrics collection, and trace visualization.
When an agent’s code caused an error, the agent
could read the stack trace, examine the logs, and
trace the request through the system.
This is not luxury infrastructure. Without
observability, an agent that generates buggy code
enters a debugging loop without information. It
guesses, modifies, reruns, and guesses again. With
observability, it reads the error trace, identifies the
line, understands the cause, and fixes the right
thing.
The combination of these three capabilities means
that agents operate in an environment as rich as
what a human developer experiences. They can
write code, run it, see what happens, read the
errors, and fix the problems. That tight feedback
loop is what makes autonomous code generation
viable at production scale.
Without application legibility, agents write code
blind. With legibility, they write, test, observe, and
iterate, just like a human developer would.
Source: OpenAI’s “Harness engineering” post:
bootable instances per git worktree so Codex can
launch one per change with its own logs and
metrics; Chrome DevTools Protocol wired into the
agent runtime so Codex can drive the UI and
reason about DOM snapshots/screenshots; local
observability stack per worktree with
LogQL/PromQL access for logs and metrics.
Harness Engineering Pattern 4 (from
OpenAI’s experiment): Mechanical
Architecture Enforcement
Trust but verify is good advice for hiring. It is
terrible advice for agents.
OpenAI’s fourth harness engineering pattern is
mechanical enforcement of architectural
constraints - a rigid architectural model per
business domain with strictly validated
dependency directions, enforced via custom
linters and structural tests. Not guidelines. Not
suggestions. Not comments in a
CONVENTIONS.md file that agents might read and
might follow. Actual linters that run automatically
and block merges when violated.
OpenAI harness engineering: Mechanical Enforcement
Here is the key insight: linters must include
remediation instructions.
A traditional linter says “naming convention
violated on line 47.” That is useful for a human who
knows the naming convention and just made a
typo. It is useless for an agent that has never seen
the convention and does not know what the
correct name should be.
What Actionable Linter Output Looks Like
OpenAI’s custom linters output structured
feedback designed for agent consumption:
ERROR: Naming convention violation
File: modules/billing/src/invoice_handler.py
Line: 47
Found: `processInvoice`
Expected pattern: snake_case function names
Fix: Rename to `process_invoice`
Reference: See modules/billing/CONVENTIONS.md#namin
That structured error message is not just
informative. It is actionable. The agent reads it,
understands exactly what went wrong, and fixes it
without human intervention. The linter becomes a
feedback loop, not just a gate.
Without the “Fix” line, the agent knows something
is wrong but must infer the correction. With the
“Fix” line, the agent has an instruction. The
difference in first-try success rates is significant.
What Gets Mechanically Enforced
OpenAI used four categories of automated
enforcement:
Naming conventions
What it checks: Function, class, variable names
match module patterns
Agent response: Auto-rename
Architecture boundaries
What it checks: No cross-module imports that
violate dependency rules
Agent response: Restructure imports
API contract compliance
What it checks: Endpoints match OpenAPI spec,
request/response schemas validated
Agent response: Fix schemas
Test coverage thresholds
What it checks: New code must include tests,
minimum coverage per module
Agent response: Generate missing tests
OpenAI harness engineering: Automated Enforcement
Each category includes machine-readable
remediation instructions. The agent runs the
linter, reads the failures, applies the fixes, and
runs the linter again. This loop continues until all
checks pass.
The humans never see the violations. They only
see the clean result. That is the whole point.
This is CI/CD principles applied to agentic
development workflows. The same logic that
makes automated test suites valuable for humans
makes automated linters with remediation
instructions valuable for agents. The mechanism is
the same. The consumer of the feedback is
different.
Source: OpenAI’s “Harness engineering” post -
rigid architectural model per business domain
with strictly validated dependency directions,
enforced via custom linters and structural tests.
The specific linter examples above are consistent
with OpenAI’s description of custom lints with
remediation instructions, generalized here as a
pattern.
OpenAI harness engineering: Dependency Stack requires constraints
Harness Engineering Pattern 5 (from
OpenAI’s experiment): Throughput
Changes Merge Philosophy
This is the pattern that surprised me most, and it is
the one most teams will resist.
OpenAI harness engineering: Througput Merge Philosophy
In traditional development, blocking quality gates
make sense. You have a finite number of
developers. Each pull request gets careful review.
Merging is a deliberate, gated act. Speed matters,
but correctness matters more, because fixing a bad
merge is expensive.
When you have dozens of agents producing
hundreds of pull requests per day, that math
changes completely.
The Throughput Problem at Agentic Scale
Consider the arithmetic. A team of 5 developers
can review approximately 15 to 25 PRs per day with
reasonable thoroughness. That is the human
review ceiling.
An agentic workflow with 20 concurrent agents
can produce 40 to 80 PRs per day. You cannot scale
human review to match that rate. If every PR waits
for a human reviewer, you have recreated the exact
constraint you were trying to eliminate. The agents
can write code in parallel all day long, but if merge
is sequential and human-gated, your throughput is
limited by the speed of human review.
The New Merge Philosophy
OpenAI shifted to a fundamentally different
approach.
Merge fast. Detect fast. Fix fast.
OpenAI describes minimal blocking merge gates
and short-lived PRs. This article generalizes that
into a “merge fast, detect fast, fix fast” philosophy
for high-throughput agent workflows.
Instead of trying to prevent every possible problem
before merge, they built systems to detect and
remediate problems after merge. The linters still
run pre-merge. The tests still run pre-merge. But
the review process changed from “block until a
human approves” to “merge if all automated
checks pass, flag for human review async.”
Why This Works (and Why It Only Works with the
Other Harness Patterns)
This philosophy only holds because of the previous
four patterns. The repo has complete
documentation (Pattern 1). The structure is
navigable (Pattern 2). The application is fully
observable (Pattern 3). Architectural constraints
are mechanically enforced (Pattern 4). With all
four in place, the risk of a bad automated merge is
low enough that speed wins over caution.
Remove any one of those patterns and the fast-
merge philosophy becomes a liability. Agents
merging fast without observability means bad code
reaches production before anyone knows it is bad.
Agents merging fast without architectural
enforcement means module boundaries erode at
high speed.
The patterns are interdependent. They are a
system.
This does not mean abandoning quality. It means
moving quality enforcement from human gates to
automated systems. The standards do not drop.
The mechanism for enforcing them changes.
Source: OpenAI describes minimal blocking merge
gates and short-lived PRs. This article generalizes
that into a “merge fast, detect fast, fix fast”
philosophy for high-throughput agent workflows.
Also informed by Rohit’s harness engineering
thread on context engineering, constraints, and
feedback loops.
The Engineering Role Transformation
Let me be direct about what I think this means for
engineering teams, because too many articles
dance around it. Based on OpenAI’s experiment
and similar agentic setups, I argue the engineer’s
role shifts from code production to harness design
and environment engineering - but this is my
interpretation, not official OpenAI guidance.
The engineers at OpenAI did not become less
skilled during this experiment. They became
differently skilled. The role shifted from
producing code to producing environments that
produce code.
OpenAI harness engineering: Harness Engineer’s Clock
Here is what that looks like day to day.
Morning: Review overnight agent PRs. Check
monitoring dashboards for drift. Update the brief
map file with new context about today’s priorities.
Midday: Design a new module. Write the README,
CONVENTIONS.md, and API spec. Define the
linter rules. Set up the isolated test environment.
The agent will do the implementation.
Afternoon: Investigate a failure pattern. Three
agents hit the same error. Trace it to a missing
convention in the billing module’s documentation.
Add the convention. Write a linter rule to catch it.
Watch agents self-correct on the next run.
End of day: Review metrics. How many PRs passed
first try? How many needed linter iterations?
Where are agents spending the most cycles? What
documentation gaps are causing the most failures?
OpenAI harness engineering: Activities
Notice what is missing from that schedule. Coding.
The engineers are designing, reviewing,
investigating, and improving, but they are not
writing code. They are building and maintaining
the harness.
This is not a theoretical future. OpenAI
demonstrated it at scale. Over five months. With
more than a million lines of working code.
What This Means for the Harness
Engineering Framework
Rohit’s harness engineering framework maps to
the OpenAI experiment almost perfectly. Each of
his layers has a direct analog:
Context Engineering: Repository-as-truth + brief
map files
Architectural Constraints: Custom linters with
remediation instructions
Garbage Collection: Post-merge monitoring +
agent auto-fix
Feedback Loops: Linter iteration cycles +
observability stack
Observability: Full traces, structured logs,
metrics dashboards
The OpenAI experiment validates the framework
empirically. You do not need to guess whether
harness engineering works at scale. You can point
to a million lines of agent-written code and say:
this is what happens when you take the harness
seriously.
OpenAI harness engineering: How does this map to Rohit’s Harness
Framework
Practical Takeaways
If you are building agentic development
workflows, here are five concrete actions to take
this week.
1. Audit your repo for invisible knowledge. Walk
through your onboarding docs. Everything a new
hire needs to learn should be in the repository. If it
is in Slack, Confluence, or someone’s head, move it
to the repo. Quantify the gap: how many things
would break if you hired an agent today with only
your repo as context?
2. Create a brief map file. Start with a single
BRIEF.md at the root. List every module, its
purpose, its current state, and where to find
deeper documentation. Keep it under 200 lines. If
you cannot summarize your codebase in 200 lines,
the problem is not the line limit.
3. Add remediation instructions to your linters.
For every lint rule, add a “Fix” line that tells the
agent exactly what to do. “Naming violation” is not
enough. “Rename processInvoice to
process_invoice per
modules/billing/CONVENTIONS.md#naming" is
what agents need. The time investment here pays
back immediately in reduced linter iteration
cycles.
4. Set up isolated dev instances. Even if you start
with Docker Compose, give each agent session its
own environment. Shared environments create
race conditions that waste agent cycles and
produce confusing failures. Isolation is table stakes
for parallel agent work.
5. Rethink your merge gates. If automated checks
are comprehensive enough, consider reducing
blocking human review to high-risk changes only.
Let agents merge low-risk, well-tested changes
automatically. Track the percentage of agent PRs
that require human intervention. Drive that
number down by improving the harness, not by
adding more human review.
Discussion Questions
If your team adopted zero-manual-code
tomorrow, which of the five harness engineering
patterns would be the hardest to implement, and
why?
What knowledge currently lives outside your
repository that would break agents if they had to
rely only on your codebase?
Does moving quality enforcement from human
gates to mechanical linters raise the floor, lower
the ceiling, or both, for code quality in your
organization?
OpenAI harness engineering: Moving from coding to engineering
factories

In , we zoom out from individual implementations
to the full ecosystem. The agent harness space has
organized itself into a seven-layer taxonomy, from
human oversight platforms down to individual
coding agents. Understanding where each tool fits
in that taxonomy is the difference between
assembling a coherent stack and buying seven
tools that all do the same thing while leaving
critical gaps in your pipeline.
